text.title: discriminated-union-output Core Rules
text.summary: Four testable rules for discriminated unions in structured output.

rule.statement: The discriminator field MUST be the first field declared in every branch model and MUST be typed as `Literal["<unique-value>"]`.
rule.rationale: Constrained decoding locks the schema branch only once the discriminator literal is emitted; placing it first means subsequent fields are constrained from the very next token onward.

rule.statement: Combine branches with `Annotated[A | B | C, Field(discriminator="kind")]`; never rely on plain `A | B | C` smart-union mode for production schemas.
rule.rationale: Plain unions emit `anyOf` without a discriminator keyword. OpenAI strict mode then has to try every branch; with overlapping fields this either fails to compile or accepts cross-branch data.

rule.statement: If two branches share more than ~90% of their fields, collapse them into one model with a literal `kind` field plus channel-specific optional fields, NOT into a discriminated union.
rule.rationale: Two near-identical branches double the schema size, double the prompt-cache footprint, and force the model to learn two shapes for one concept. Collapse recovers cache efficiency and reduces routing noise.

rule.statement: If the set of branches is open (user-defined types, plug-in actions), use a registry pattern with a free-string `kind` and a separate validation pass, NOT a discriminated union.
rule.rationale: Discriminated unions need a closed enum of branches. An open set defeats the constraint and forces ad-hoc handling for unknown kinds; a registry encodes the openness explicitly.
